From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American hip-hop magazine

*XXL*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |

| Staff writers | Ayabulela Tshofela |

| Publisher | [Townsquare Media](/wiki/Townsquare_Media "Townsquare Media") |
| Founded | 1997 |
| Country | United States |
| Based in | [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") |
| Language | English |
| Website | [xxlmag.com](http://xxlmag.com) |
| [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") | [1093-0647](https://www.worldcat.org/search?fq=x0:jrnl&q=n2:1093-0647) |

***XXL***[[note 1]](#cite_note-2) is an American [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") magazine, published by [Townsquare Media](/wiki/Townsquare_Media "Townsquare Media"), founded in 1997 by [James Bernard](/wiki/James_Bernard_(journalist) "James Bernard (journalist)") and Reginald Dennis, both former co-editors of The Source Magazine.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In August 1997,[[2]](#cite_note-3) [Harris Publications](/wiki/Harris_Publications "Harris Publications") released the first issue of *XXL*. It featured rappers [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") and [Master P](/wiki/Master_P "Master P") on a double cover.[[3]](#cite_note-xxlmag1-4) In December 2006, *XXL* took over the struggling hip-hop producer and DJ magazine *[Scratch](/wiki/Scratch_(magazine) "Scratch (magazine)")* (another publication owned by Harris Publications), re-branding it as *XXL Presents Scratch Magazine*. However *Scratch* shut down less than a year later in September 2007.

Other titles with limited runs have been launched under the *XXL* brand, including *Hip-Hop Soul*, *Eye Candy* and *Shade 45*. *XXL* has released many other special projects, including tour programs, mixtapes, and exclusive DVDs. *XXL* also maintains a popular website, which provides daily hip-hop news, original content, and content from the magazine.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

In 2014, [Townsquare Media](/wiki/Townsquare_Media "Townsquare Media") acquired *XXL*,[[5]](#cite_note-bill-6) *[King](/wiki/King_(magazine) "King (magazine)")* and *Antenna* from Harris Publications.

On October 14, 2014, Townsquare announced it would continue print publication of *XXL*.[[6]](#cite_note-7) In December 2014 the company reported that the magazine would be published on a quarterly basis.[[5]](#cite_note-bill-6) Later it was changed from a quarterly basis to a monthly basis.

### Past editors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Past editors")]

The magazine's past editors include Reginald C. Dennis (formerly of *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)")*), Sheena Lester (former editor-in-chief of *RapPages* and music editor at *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")*), Elliott Wilson (formerly of *The Beat-Down Newspaper*, *[Ego Trip](/wiki/Ego_trip_(magazine) "Ego trip (magazine)")* and *The Source*)[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) and Datwon Thomas (former editor-in-chief of *[King](/wiki/King_(magazine) "King (magazine)"))*.

In May 2009, Datwon Thomas resigned from *XXL* and executive editor Vanessa Satten, who had been with *XXL* since 1998, was named the new editor-in-chief.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

### Special releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Special releases")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/9/92/XXL_Presents_Shade_45_magazine_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:XXL_Presents_Shade_45_magazine_cover.jpg)

Magazine cover of *XXL Presents Shade 45*

The magazine commissioned *[A Great Day in Hip Hop](/wiki/A_Great_Day_in_Hip_Hop "A Great Day in Hip Hop"),* which is a black-and-white photograph of over 200 hip-hop artists and producers in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem,_Manhattan "Harlem, Manhattan"), New York, taken by photographer [Gordon Parks](/wiki/Gordon_Parks "Gordon Parks") on September 29, 1998.[[10]](#cite_note-11) It was commissioned by the magazine as a homage to [Art Kane](/wiki/Art_Kane "Art Kane")'s *[A Great Day in Harlem](/wiki/A_Great_Day_in_Harlem_(photograph) "A Great Day in Harlem (photograph)")*, photographed in 1958.[[11]](#cite_note-vice-12)

In August 2005, [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem") and *XXL* teamed up to release a special issue titled *XXL Presents Shade 45*, designed to simultaneously give maximum exposure to his radio station [Shade 45](/wiki/Shade_45 "Shade 45"), the station's associated radio DJs, the [Shady Records](/wiki/Shady_Records "Shady Records") label as a whole, and [G-Unit Records](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records")' artists. *XXL* executive publisher Jonathan Rheingold stated that typically magazines based around particular artists were not favorable, but "since Shade 45 is a truly authentic and uncensored rap radio channel, the marriage with the *XXL* brand made sense," feeling that it would interest rap fans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In September 2006, *XXL* released a 90-minute DVD titled *XXL DVD Magazine Vol. 1*, which featured exclusive interviews and content with rappers such as [50 Cent](/wiki/50_Cent "50 Cent"), [Ice Cube](/wiki/Ice_Cube "Ice Cube"), [Fat Joe](/wiki/Fat_Joe "Fat Joe"), [Paul Wall](/wiki/Paul_Wall "Paul Wall"), and [Mike Jones](/wiki/Mike_Jones_(rapper) "Mike Jones (rapper)").[[12]](#cite_note-13)

In November 2008, *XXL* released *XXL Raps Volume 1*, which included music from 50 Cent, [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit"), [Common](/wiki/Common_(rapper) "Common (rapper)"), [Jim Jones](/wiki/Jim_Jones_(rapper) "Jim Jones (rapper)"), and [Fabolous](/wiki/Fabolous "Fabolous").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On August 20, 2013, *XXL* marked its sixteenth anniversary by releasing its 150th issue, which featured the first solo cover on the magazine from [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(rapper) "Drake (rapper)"), along with rappers such as [Kendrick Lamar](/wiki/Kendrick_Lamar "Kendrick Lamar") and [B.o.B](/wiki/B.o.B "B.o.B") reviewing classic albums.[[3]](#cite_note-xxlmag1-4)

## Annual Freshman Class list

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Annual Freshman Class list")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section's **tone or style may not reflect the [encyclopedic tone](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") used on Wikipedia**. See Wikipedia's [guide to writing better articles](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") for suggestions. *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Beginning in 2007 (skipping 2008),[[13]](#cite_note-14) *XXL* releases its annual "Freshman Class" list. The issue features 10-12 artists-to-watch, all appearing on the cover of the magazine. The list has a history of showcasing unknown/underground rappers, as well as artists considered to be on the rise. The list creates significant [marketing buzz](/wiki/Marketing_buzz "Marketing buzz") among listeners and artists alike, and is credited for giving many artists their first taste of fame.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-XXL-16)[[16]](#cite_note-XXL2-17)

### Notes

2. **[^](#cite_ref-fn2_32-0)** In 2018, there was not a 10th spot pick as [Lil Skies](/wiki/Lil_Skies "Lil Skies") and his replacement, [Rich the Kid](/wiki/Rich_the_Kid "Rich the Kid") both declined the list.[[28]](#cite_note-Skies-30)[[29]](#cite_note-Rich-31)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-fn3_38-0)** In 2020, [Pop Smoke](/wiki/Pop_Smoke "Pop Smoke") was the first freshman selected for the list, but died before attending the photoshoot.[[33]](#cite_note-xxlmag2-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)

### Additions to the list

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Additions to the list")]

Occasionally, the Freshman Class list may contain extra additions to include more rappers. The 2011, 2013, 2019, 2021 and 2024 Freshman Class lists, for example, had 11 rappers. In the case for the 2013 list, *XXL* added an honorary extra spot for [Chief Keef](/wiki/Chief_Keef "Chief Keef") due to the artist being in a six-day jail stint and therefore being unable to attend the photo shoot in New York City.[[22]](#cite_note-XXL_Chief_Keef-24) For the 2025 list, [EBK Jaaybo](/wiki/EBK_Jaaybo "EBK Jaaybo"), who was the 10th spot winner, was arrested prior to the photo shoot resulting in him being unable to attend. However, he was still officially included on the list.[[40]](#cite_note-XXL_2025-44) In 2014, 2020, 2022, 2023 and 2025, the Freshman Class lists included 12 rappers.

### Artists that declined the cover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=XXL_(magazine)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Artists that declined the cover")]

For most Freshman covers, after the list has been announced, an artist will come out and say that they were offered a spot on the cover by *XXL* but chose to decline it. Other times, *XXL* themselves will reveal someone that they asked to be on the cover and were directly told by the artist that they were not interested. Below is a list of rappers who have declined the offer of being an *XXL* Freshman, and the various reasons why they declined.

| Year | Artist(s) | Reason | Ref. |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2010 | [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") | Respectfully declined and felt they were chosen too late in their respective careers. In an interview with MTV, Nicki Minaj spoke on why she and Drake declined the cover by saying "With all due respect, we felt like we kinda had graduated from the Freshman Class. We felt *XXL* missed the mark when they didn't put us on the cover prior to that (2009). They have to pay for that". | [[41]](#cite_note-:0-45) |
| [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj") |
| 2011 | [Vado](/wiki/Vado_(rapper) "Vado (rapper)") | In an interview with Shade 45, Cam'ron revealed that he spoke to the Editor-In-Chief Vanessa Satten about putting Vado on the 2011 issue. However, Cam'ron wanted Vado to be the center focus and not share the cover with multiple other artists. When Vanessa revealed that she couldn't guarantee that Vado would be the "main highlight" of the cover, Cam'ron respectfully declined the offer on behalf of Vado. |
| [Tyler, the Creator](/wiki/Tyler,_the_Creator "Tyler, the Creator") | Respectfully declined. |
| 2012 | [ASAP Rocky](/wiki/ASAP_Rocky "ASAP Rocky") | Respectfully declined. Schedule was too tight due to [touring](/wiki/Club_Paradise_Tour "Club Paradise Tour"). |
| 2014 | [Young Thug](/wiki/Young_Thug "Young Thug") | On Sway's Universe, Vanessa Satten revealed that the day before the photoshoot took place, someone from Young Thug's camp called *XXL* at 10:30 pm and directly told them that he wasn't coming to the shoot the next day. |
| 2015 | [ILoveMakonnen](/wiki/ILoveMakonnen "ILoveMakonnen") | Not responsive to *XXL*. |
| [PartyNextDoor](/wiki/PartyNextDoor "PartyNextDoor") |
| 2016 | [Tory Lanez](/wiki/Tory_Lanez "Tory Lanez") | On April 15, 2016, Tory Lanez (in a since-deleted tweet) said on Twitter "I passed on the *XXL* cover. Don't b confused". Although he didn't give an official reason, it is believed that he felt he was chosen too late in his career. | [[42]](#cite_note-46) |
| [Post Malone](/wiki/Post_Malone "Post Malone") | Stated he was tired and was not up to flying to New York for the photoshoot. However, *XXL* editor-in-chief Vanessa Satten claimed Malone's publicist stated that Malone did not want to be boxed in as a hip-hop artist. | [[41]](#cite_note-:0-45) |
| 2017 | [Young M.A](/wiki/Young_M.A "Young M.A") | Felt beyond the list. She asked to have a solo cover but they disagreed and said that she needed more attention to her name before that could happen. | [[43]](#cite_note-allhiphop-47) |
| [YFN Lucci](/wiki/YFN_Lucci "YFN Lucci") | YFN Lucci initially refused to play music for *XXL* and said he didn't want to play any music if he wasn't guaranteed a spot on the list. He later changed his mind days before the shoot but it was too late. |
| [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B") | Although not technically a "declined offer", *XXL* stated that Cardi B wasn't selected to be a freshman because they weren't sure if Cardi was leaning more towards having a career in television or in music. |
| [Famous Dex](/wiki/Famous_Dex "Famous Dex") | *XXL* stood away from adding Famous Dex after there was evidence shown that he [beat up his girlfriend](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women"). They further spoke on XXXTentacion's addition to the 2017 list (as it was controversial), saying that although he was accused of beating up his ex-girlfriend, the incident was not caught on camera, XXXTentacion had not been found guilty, and that he was arguably having a major influence on his generation. | [[43]](#cite_note-allhiphop-47)[[44]](#cite_note-xxlmag-48) |
| 2018 | [Lil Skies](/wiki/Lil_Skies "Lil Skies") | On [Instagram Live](/wiki/Instagram_Live "Instagram Live"), he explained that he respectfully declined due to *XXL* apparently telling him that he would be the 10th Spot Freshman before the voting process even began.
